title: 30-Year Term Life Insurance Canada (2026)
seo_title: 30-Year Term Life Insurance Canada | Lock in Your Rate | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Get 30-year term life insurance in Canada. Lock in your rate for 30 years from $35/month. Best for young families and 30-year mortgages. Free quotes.

secondary_keywords: best term life insurance for young families
_meta.page_role: Product page targeting very young families and 30-year mortgage holders wanting maximum rate lock

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use '30-year term' or '30-year term life insurance' 3–5 times per 500 words. Vary with synonyms below.
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: A 28-year-old with a newborn who locks in a 30-year rate is covered until age 58 — through their entire working life and all of their child's dependent years.
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not make it sound like the only good option. Be honest: 20-year is often enough. Position 30-year for specific situations.

_writer_notes.focus: Maximum protection, maximum rate lock — the longest affordable term
key_points: Lead with: locks in your rate for 30 years — protection that outlasts your mortgage and covers your children all the way to adulthood
Target buyer: parents of newborns and infants, new 30-year mortgage holders, people in their late 20s
Breadcrumb: Home > Life Insurance > Term Life Insurance > 30-Year Term

_writer_notes.focus: Clear product definition — emphasize the 30-year rate lock as a key benefit
key_points: DEFINITION: Fixed coverage and fixed premiums for exactly 30 years. The longest affordable term length before permanent insurance.
RATE LOCK: Your premium is locked in from day one and never changes for 30 years regardless of health changes or age.
GUARANTEED COVERAGE: As long as you pay your premium, you're covered — no re-qualification, no medical exams during the term.
CONVERTIBLE: Most 30-year term policies can be converted to permanent insurance without a medical exam if your needs change.
VALUE: Per dollar of coverage, 30-year term costs more than 10 or 20-year term — but significantly less than whole life, and you're locking in today's rates for a very long period.

page_data.title: The Power of Locking In Your Rate
page_data.text: A 28-year-old buying 30-year term locks in their rate until age 58. That means three decades of coverage at today's prices — no matter what health changes occur in that time. If you develop diabetes, heart disease, or cancer during your term, your coverage and premium are completely unaffected.

page_data.heading: Who Should Buy 30-Year Term Life Insurance?

_writer_notes.focus: Very specific audience — newborn parents, 30-yr mortgage holders, late-20s buyers
key_points: 1. PARENTS OF NEWBORNS/INFANTS: A 30-year term covers your child from birth until age 30. Ensures your family is protected through their entire dependent years.
2. 30-YEAR MORTGAGE HOLDERS: If you just signed a 30-year mortgage, a 30-year term runs exactly alongside it — your family can always pay off the home.
3. AGES 25–35: The younger you buy, the cheaper the 30-year rate. A 28-year-old's 30-year term is far more affordable than a 38-year-old buying 20-year.
4. THOSE WHO WANT TO 'SET AND FORGET': Buy once at a young age and you're covered until nearly retirement. No need to re-shop.
5. HEALTH RISK AWARENESS: If there is family history of illness (cancer, heart disease), locking in now for 30 years may be the safest financial decision.
WHO 30-YEAR IS NOT FOR: Ages 45+ (coverage would extend past when most people need it), those who just want the cheapest premium, or those with very short-term obligations.

page_data.heading: How Much Does 30-Year Term Life Insurance Cost?
paragraphs: 30-year term costs more per month than 10 or 20-year term — but the longer rate lock provides exceptional value, especially for young buyers.

_writer_notes.focus: Contextualise: yes it costs more than 20-year, but look at what you're buying
key_points: Write 2-3 sentences intro: 30-year term premiums are roughly 30-40% higher than 20-year for the same coverage and age
But: note that if you buy at 30, your 30-year term costs less than buying a 20-year term at age 40
Note: 30-year term is generally only available up to about age 55 at most Canadian insurers
After table: explain locking in at 30 vs 35 — the 5-year difference compounds significantly over 30 years
STAT to include: The extra $15–20/month between 20-year and 30-year term adds up to $5,400–$7,200 over the life of the term, but covers you for 10 additional critical years

page_data.title: $500,000 — 30-Year Term Monthly Premiums
page_data.footnote: Sample rates from top Canadian insurers, 2026. Most insurers cap 30-year term at age 55. Non-smoker = nicotine-free for 12 months.

page_data.title: Calculate Your 30-Year Term Life Insurance Cost

page_data.heading: 30-Year Term vs 20-Year Term: Should You Pay Extra?

_writer_notes.focus: Honest comparison — when is extra 10-year coverage worth it and when isn't it?
key_points: COST DIFFERENCE (age 35): 20-year = $32–50/mo vs 30-year = $45–70/mo. Extra cost: ~$15–20/month.
IS IT WORTH IT? The answer comes down to: how old are your children, and how long is your mortgage?
WORTH IT scenario: 28-year-old with newborn, just signed 30-year mortgage — 30-year term is the clear answer
NOT WORTH IT scenario: 40-year-old whose kids are 10 and 13, mortgage has 15 years left — 20-year term is plenty
ALTERNATIVE: Buy 20-year term now + plan to reassess coverage in 10 years if still needed

page_data.title: 30-Year vs 20-Year Term: Is the Extra Cost Worth It?
rows: Coverage runs to age 58 — covers child to adulthood and full mortgage
30-year covers kids to age 33/36; 20-year covers to 23/26 — both reasonable
Kids near-adult by end of term; mortgage covered with margin
No need for 30-year; 20-year matches obligations exactly
Lock in healthy rates now before potential health changes
page_data.footnote: When in doubt, get quotes for both 20-year and 30-year terms. The premium difference is usually smaller than expected.

page_data.heading: Compare 30-Year Term Life Insurance Rates
page_data.body: Lock in your rate today before your next birthday. Compare quotes from 20+ Canadian insurers — free and no obligation.

items.question: Is 30-year term life insurance worth it?
items.answer: For parents of young children (under age 5) or those with a 30-year mortgage, yes — it provides invaluable peace of mind that your family is protected for three full decades at a locked-in rate. For those with older children or shorter obligations, 20-year term may be sufficient.

items.question: Up to what age can I buy 30-year term life insurance?
items.answer: Most Canadian insurers offer 30-year term up to age 55. Some may cap at age 50. If you're over 55, you may be limited to 10 or 20-year terms, Term-to-100, or permanent life insurance.

items.question: How does 30-year term compare to whole life insurance?
items.answer: 30-year term is typically 8–15x cheaper than whole life for the same death benefit. Whole life provides lifetime coverage and builds cash value. 30-year term expires but provides maximum protection during your most financially critical decades at a fraction of the cost.

items.question: Can I cancel my 30-year term policy?
items.answer: Yes, you can cancel anytime. Since term life has no cash value, you won't receive any money back for premiums paid. You simply stop paying and the policy lapses after the grace period.

items.question: What happens at the end of 30-year term life insurance?
items.answer: At the end of 30 years, you have four options: let the policy expire (if no longer needed), renew at your current age rates (no medical exam, significantly higher premium), convert to permanent insurance (no medical exam, before deadline), or apply for a new term policy (requires new underwriting).
